title: Attention Is All You Need
author: Vaswani et al. (2017)
gist: The Transformer replaces recurrence and convolution entirely with attention: every position in a sequence attends directly to every other, so long-range dependencies cost one step instead of many, and training parallelizes across the whole sequence. Stacked self-attention and feed-forward layers — multi-headed, so the model can attend to different kinds of relations at once — reach 28.4 BLEU on WMT English–German and 41.8 on English–French, a new state of the art at the time, after days rather than weeks of training, and generalize cleanly to tasks like constituency parsing.

segments.text: Before 2017, sequence transduction ran on recurrent or convolutional encoder–decoders. The core obstacles: recurrence computes a hidden state per position, each depending on the previous one, which forbids parallelizing within a sequence; and even the convolutional escape routes (ByteNet, ConvS2S) need a number of operations that grows with the distance between two positions, making long-range dependencies progressively harder to learn. Attention could already relate arbitrary positions in constant steps — but it had only ever ridden along on top of recurrent models, connecting encoder to decoder. The Transformer's bet is that attention is sufficient by itself.

The architecture keeps the encoder–decoder outline and swaps the machinery: six identical layers per side, each pairing multi-head self-attention with a position-wise feed-forward network, wrapped in residual connections and layer normalization. Attention is scaled dot-product — queries against keys, damped by the square root of the key dimension, softmaxed onto values — run eight ways in parallel over learned projections so different heads can watch different representation subspaces. The decoder masks its self-attention to preserve autoregression and adds a sublayer attending over the encoder's output; sinusoidal positional encodings inject order, since nothing else in the model knows position exists.

Why prefer self-attention? Three measurements: computation per layer, minimum sequential operations (parallelism), and the length of the path a signal travels between distant positions — self-attention makes that path constant-length where recurrence needs one step per intervening token and convolution needs a whole stack of layers. Training is deliberately unexotic: WMT 2014 data with sub-word vocabularies, eight P100 GPUs on one machine, Adam with a warmup-then-decay schedule, dropout, and label smoothing. The base model trains in twelve hours; the big one in three and a half days.

The big model reaches 28.4 BLEU on English–German — more than 2 BLEU over everything previously reported, ensembles included — and 41.8 on English–French, at a fraction of competitors' training cost. Ablations map the design space: eight heads beat one and beat sixteen, bigger models win, dropout is essential, and learned positional embeddings buy nothing over sinusoids. A four-layer Transformer also lands near state of the art on English constituency parsing without task-specific tuning. The conclusion sketches what came next for the whole field: attention beyond text — images, audio, video — and less sequential generation.

segments.heading: Abstract
segments.text: The dominant sequence transduction models combine recurrent or convolutional encoder–decoders with an attention bridge. This paper proposes the Transformer, built on attention alone, with no recurrence or convolution: simpler, more parallelizable, and faster to train. On WMT 2014 English-to-German it reaches 28.4 BLEU, more than 2 BLEU beyond the best previous results including ensembles; on English-to-French it sets a single-model record of 41.8 BLEU after 3.5 days on eight GPUs, a small fraction of the literature's training costs. It also generalizes: English constituency parsing works well with both large and limited training data.

segments.heading: 1 Introduction
segments.text: Recurrent networks — LSTMs and gated RNNs above all — have been the state of the art for language modeling and translation, but their defining computation is sequential: one hidden state per position, each conditioned on the last. That precludes parallelization within a training example, which becomes critical at longer sequence lengths where memory limits batching. Factorization tricks and conditional computation improve efficiency without lifting the fundamental constraint. Attention mechanisms let models relate positions regardless of distance, but have almost always been used alongside a recurrent network. The Transformer dispenses with recurrence entirely, drawing global dependencies between input and output with attention alone — and reaches new state-of-the-art translation quality after as little as twelve hours on eight P100 GPUs.

segments.heading: 2 Background
segments.text: Reducing sequential computation also motivates Extended Neural GPU, ByteNet, and ConvS2S, which compute all positions in parallel with convolutions — but relating two arbitrary positions there takes a number of operations that grows with their distance, linearly for ConvS2S and logarithmically for ByteNet, which makes distant dependencies harder to learn. The Transformer cuts this to a constant number of operations, paying with reduced effective resolution that multi-head attention counteracts. Self-attention — attention relating positions of a single sequence to each other — had already proven itself in reading comprehension, summarization, and entailment; end-to-end memory networks used recurrent attention instead of sequence-aligned recurrence. The Transformer is the first transduction model relying entirely on self-attention, without sequence-aligned RNNs or convolution.

segments.heading: 3 Model Architecture
segments.text: The encoder stacks six identical layers, each a multi-head self-attention sublayer plus a position-wise feed-forward network, both wrapped in residual connections followed by layer normalization; all sublayers produce dimension 512. The decoder mirrors this and adds a third sublayer attending over encoder output, with its self-attention masked so position i sees only earlier positions. Attention is scaled dot-product: queries dotted with keys, divided by the square root of the key dimension — without which large dot products push softmax into vanishing-gradient territory — then softmaxed onto values. Multi-head attention runs eight such attentions in parallel over learned projections, letting the model jointly attend to different representation subspaces; heads are cheap because each is proportionally smaller. The same mechanism serves three roles — encoder self-attention, masked decoder self-attention, and encoder–decoder attention — alongside a per-position two-layer feed-forward network with inner dimension 2048, embedding matrices shared with the output softmax, and sinusoidal positional encodings chosen over learned ones for their chance of extrapolating to unseen lengths.

segments.heading: 4 Why Self-Attention
segments.text: Three desiderata drive the comparison with recurrent and convolutional layers: total computation per layer, the minimum number of sequential operations (a proxy for parallelism), and the path length between long-range dependencies — the shorter the path between any two positions, the easier the dependency is to learn. Self-attention connects all positions in a constant number of sequential operations where recurrence needs O(n); per layer, it is faster than recurrence whenever sequence length is smaller than representation dimensionality, which is the common case for sentence-piece representations. A single convolution with kernel width smaller than the sequence doesn't even connect all pairs, and a full stack costs a factor of the kernel width more. Restricting attention to a neighborhood is left as future work for very long sequences. A side benefit: attention distributions are inspectable, and individual heads turn out to specialize in recognizably syntactic and semantic behavior.

segments.heading: 5 Training
segments.text: Data: WMT 2014 English–German (4.5 million sentence pairs, byte-pair encoded into a shared 37k-token vocabulary) and the far larger English–French (36 million pairs, 32k word-pieces), with batches grouped by approximate length, about 25k source and 25k target tokens each. Hardware: a single machine with eight P100 GPUs — base models take 100k steps at 0.4 seconds each, twelve hours; the big model takes 300k steps at one second each, 3.5 days. Optimization is Adam with a learning rate that warms up linearly for 4,000 steps and then decays with the inverse square root of the step number. Regularization: dropout of 0.1 on every sublayer output and on the embedding-plus-positional-encoding sums, and label smoothing of 0.1 — which costs perplexity but buys accuracy and BLEU.

segments.heading: 6 Results
segments.text: On WMT 2014 English-to-German the big Transformer reaches 28.4 BLEU, more than 2.0 beyond all previously reported models including ensembles, at a fraction of their training cost — even the base model beats every prior single model. On English-to-French the big model reaches 41.8, outperforming all previous single models at less than a quarter of their cost. Inference uses beam search with beam 4 and length penalty 0.6, over averaged checkpoints. The ablation table maps the design: single-head attention is 0.9 BLEU worse than eight heads, but too many heads also hurt; shrinking the attention key size hurts, suggesting dot-product compatibility is not trivially replaceable; bigger models are better; dropout is very helpful; learned positional embeddings perform identically to sinusoids. Finally, a four-layer Transformer applied to Penn Treebank constituency parsing outperforms all previously reported models except one — despite no task-specific tuning — and beats the BerkeleyParser even when trained on only 40k sentences.

segments.heading: 7 Conclusion
segments.text: The Transformer is the first sequence transduction model based entirely on attention, replacing the recurrent layers most common in encoder-decoder architectures with multi-headed self-attention. For translation it trains significantly faster than architectures based on recurrent or convolutional layers, and on both WMT 2014 tasks it achieves a new state of the art, the best model outperforming even all previously reported ensembles. The closing agenda proved prophetic: extend attention-based models to modalities beyond text — images, audio, video — investigate local, restricted attention for large inputs, and make generation less sequential. The code shipped as tensor2tensor.

segments.heading: Attention Visualizations
segments.text: Appendix figures follow individual attention heads through the encoder stack. One head in layer five carries the verb 'making' across a long gap to complete the phrase 'making...more difficult' — a long-distance dependency resolved in a single hop. Two heads in the same layer appear to perform anaphora resolution, sharpening the word 'its' onto its antecedent. Others behave like structure detectors, different heads visibly learning different tasks — concrete, inspectable evidence for the interpretability the paper claims.
